speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: My father was George H. Moody, was born in St. John, New Brunswick. He was the thirteenth child and all the rest of the family had been born in Scotland. His own mother died when he was 18 months old and his father remarried because I presumed to take care of a big family. My father, although he was so young he didn't accept it and the stepmother allowed the older children to take advantage of the younger ones. So when he was 13 he ran away from home and was on his own from then on. And he made his way to Boston and worked in a business. One of his brothers was a worker in marble in Vermont and he finally moved to Missouri. And there he opened a monument business and it was the day of big monuments and tombstones they called them. And my father apprenticed himself to his brother and learned the trade. When he was a young man he established his own monument works in Fort Scott, Kansas. But before he established his own business he was in St. Louis and I imagine he was working in such a concerned business. And he boarded with a Mrs. Manning who had been recently widowed. And her young daughter Mary Ida helped her mother and served the boarders at the table. She and my father fell in love and they were married in St. Louis, Missouri. And he took her to Fort Scott, Kansas where several of my brothers and sisters were born. There was a great desire in many young people of that time to go west. So he thought he would sell his business and go out to California leaving his family there until he was settled. And he met a man on the train who told him all about Ogden, Utah and what a boom town it was. And so he decided to get off of the train there at Ogden and see what it was like. Well he opened up a small hardware store and finally in a few months sent for my mother with five children. One, the youngest, a three month old baby. And they all had their little piece of luggage to carry. And somebody had to carry the bag for the baby and somebody had to carry the bird in the birdcage. And she came and they made their home in Ogden, Utah for several years. And I think my father was not very happy being out of his own field. So he, my mother and the family went over to Salubria, Idaho where my mother's mother had come. She had remarried and had come there and they were mining there. And my father evidently sold out his hardware business and followed them. And early in 1892 he had heard about the town up in northern Idaho, Moscow where a university had been recently established. And he thought that would be a good place to move his family so that they would have the advantage of a good education for each of them. So he came on alone to Moscow in, as I said, early in 1892 and opened a business, got in a stock of monuments and in a little building that was then on 3rd Street. And my mother followed and my father had found a house they could rent and they settled themselves in Moscow, Idaho.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And they lived in a couple of houses, I think. And my mother told about taking care of an old blind lady. And I think that was, the pay for her care was probably a pretty good part of their income at that time. And then my father had the chance to buy a house on Lynn Avenue, they called it then. I even remember the number, 722 Lynn Avenue. And he bought that place and it was a five room house. And with, I imagine it was about a half an acre adjoining it that was the garden spot. And it had fruit trees planted in it and garden space. And there in that house, I was born in January of 1894. But the year before 1893 had been the bad year in Latah County. It had rained and rained and the farmers were unable to harvest their grain. So the only monuments my father could sell was he would get on horseback and take a kit of designs and go about the country. And if he could sell a monument, he would come back home and engrave the monument in the kitchen because he'd had to give up his building downtown. And he kept his store of, his stock of marble in the barn. But he told, used to tell my, tell us about, they were just about down to the last bit of money they had. And my mother needed a sack of flour. So he went to town and purchased the flour and he had 25 cents, two bits they called it, left. And so he brought himself a two bit cigar and smoked it. But by and by things bettered themselves in Latah County and once more he opened his shop down on Main Street this time. I remember when I was a little girl he ordered the Ole Hagberg Monument which stands on the campus. There were two boys from the University of Idaho who were killed in the Philippines in the Spanish American War. And I don't know how that monument was purchased, whether it was by public, by subscription, I presume it was, by the help of the university. And when it came, of course we all wanted to go down and see it. Well, it was so long and such a long heavy box that it just about filled up his showroom. And I just thought that was just about the biggest monument that had ever been made. It stands on the University of Idaho campus now. No, if the farm was made, he didn't chisel out the farm, but he did all the lettering on it. And it stands there on the campus yet facing the northeast toward the mountains. And to me it was the most wonderful thing.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Then when I was still a little girl quite young, the administration building of the University of Idaho burned. And everybody in town seemed to go, could see the blaze in the sky and everybody went to the fire. And I was a little girl and my mother held my hand. And so I cried because she cried because the administration building was burning. And the thing I hated to have burn up was in the entry hall of the administration building, there were two glass cases. And in those cases were little artifacts that the boys who had been in the battalion over there had brought home with them. And I remember a little grass covered hut that I thought was so adorable. And I regretted, of course, the loss of those things. But the records fortunately were saved. The dean of the university saved the records. And I had a sister that year, my sister Ethel, who was a senior in college. And so the women's gymnasium was built at that time. And they were existing buildings. So the classes were held in those two buildings. But the seniors had to go across town to the Methodist Church and the city library for classes. And that was a great experience because even though it was hardship for the students, they all rallied around. And in a few years we had a new administration building. But to make use of the bricks that were left from after the fire, the university hired small boys to clean the mortar from the bricks. And my husband, Harry Sampson, was one of the small boys hired to help clean bricks. And also my brother, Morris Moody, five years older than I, worked on that too, cleaning bricks at the university. Of course, that was quite a windfall for small boys. But the beautiful old steps, the granite steps, they were in the front of the administration. And then there was a wing on either side and there were huge steps going up to each wing. So there were actually three main entrances to the administration building. Well, they thought they were of no value. So they were hauled away and they were buried where leveling was done on city lots and so on. But I can't remember the year. But the University Garden was up behind where the old administration building had been. And some class reunion decided they'd restore the old steps. And so they found enough of the old steps to build a flight of steps as they were originally up to the garden. And of course, they're there now, the old steps.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I was on the train and shipped to Moscow, Idaho. So the cost of transporting materials to his business was very expensive. And I often wonder how he did most of the lettering himself until the business became so big that he had to employ men to come in to assist him. And then he had agents who went about all over Nez Perce prairie and all around Moscow and as far as Yakima in Washington selling for him. And they would take the orders and they would carry the kits of the designs just about like that, those pictures. And people would choose what they wanted or they could come in to Moscow and see the stock on the floor. But when my father showed stock, it was always with them. Such love. And if when he was showing a special monument without thinking, his white handkerchief would come out of his pocket and he would wipe it off a bit with the old stuff. And that showed the beauty that he could see in that particular piece of stone. And he was, as a young man, he made old things for our home, that oval picture frame here hanging on the wall with my mother's and father's picture in it. He made that out of marble and how he ever could chisel that out and get it so perfect, I don't understand. And on the table there is a little marble book with my mother's initials engraved on them. And it was to represent a little white Bible. And he gave it to her as an engagement present. So I treasure my things that I have that he has made very much. There's a little book over on the table there, he's made. Brown, not marble. But later then he got, he would chip in some marble for interior use, like in bank buildings and so on, from Italy. And then he got beautiful, some beautiful green granite from Alaska. And then from Georgia, some very beautiful stone cane. But monuments in those days were inclined to be big, if possible, if the family finances warranted that. And they would sit on a base the same as the monument itself was made on. And that in turn would sit on a rough granite base. And those rough granite bases came from over here in Washington. I can't just remember where. But that was a style in those days of monument business. And after, they would have individual stones that were large. And then always at the foot would be a footstone smaller with the initials of the person who was buried there on the footstone. And often friends would ask my father to put his name someplace down on one side showing that he had engraved that particular stone. And I see many monuments in the cemetery with either G .H .M. or Moody down in one little corner at the back of the monument showing that he had engraved that monument.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was his business, did he go through periods that were harder times?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, not after that year, his business just gradually grew and grew. And then he kept having more agents out and selling more monuments. And you'd be surprised to know that the Indians were some of the very best buyers of monuments that just appealed to them. And he, when they'd come into his shop, he had rather a quiet voice. And Indians like that. They don't like to be spoken. Often we think we need to speak in louder tones to someone who is different than we are, you know, for fear they won't understand or something. But they liked him. And I know he had a namesake, an Indian baby, and named for him down on the prairie, George Moody. And afterwards I knew that baby when he grew to be a man.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Were there any epidemics or... Oh, I remember when I was a little girl, there were epidemics at one time. We had a smallpox epidemic. And we all had to be vaccinated, of course. And I was taken to Dr. Watkins' office and had my arms shut. And I was scraped with this... They used quail in those days and would scrape the skin on your arm and put the vaccine on it. And I remember I cried and another little girl laughed at me. And I never liked that little girl because she laughed because I thought it hurt. Was this Dr.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Watkins?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Uh -huh. That was Carol Riery Brinks' grandfather. And then my brother, who was five years older than I, had diphtheria at one time. But I was quite small at the time. And it seemed from what my mother told me, I played right around in the same room where he was and I didn't take it. So I don't think there was an epidemic or anything. I think it just happened to be one case. And then, of course, they quarantined the homes in those days when people had scarlet fever, diphtheria or smallpox. And there would be a sign put up at the front door quarantined in the name of the disease. And as children, of course, if we'd ever see that by somebody's front door, we'd cover our mouths with our handkerchiefs and just run by as fast as we could so we wouldn't get a germ.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you describe some of the stories in Moscow?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, we, Moscow was always quite a good trading center even in the early days. And of course, the first store, I think, was the McConnell McGuire store. And that was where the Mr. Brown's furniture store is now. The Fatuna building, we call it. And later, Mr. F .A. David was associated with that firm. And then that store went broke. And it was started again, and I don't know the year, but it was started again by, I don't know, the year. And it was to Modern Wheeler. And this advertisement doesn't give, but I can remember this store, yes, Modern Wheeler Company, the Hustlers. And they had a fall opening, and it doesn't say what year it was. But some of the prices are very interesting. Ladies' wrappers, the 75 cent ones, went at 50 cents. And the men's hats, a 75 cent hat, went at 60 cents. And they were, all during that opening week, they had specials of fruit jars and golden crown tomato cats, a regular 50 cent special at 20 cents or three for 50 cents. And all during the week, they had those specials.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: This happened before your time?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Thank you very much. We were customers of Creighton's store in regard to dry goods and clothing. And then we bought our groceries from David's store after Mr. David had started up his store. And they sent a man around each morning to your kitchen door to take orders for groceries that you might need. And then they delivered them with a horse and cart later in the day, which was a great convenience in those days because in the winter the snow was quite deep. And in the spring and fall the mud was deep, and it was a great convenience to have things brought to your door. Creighton's store had a delivery boy. And my brother Morris was their delivery boy at one time. That was after the bicycles came in. We had bicycles. And if there were several dressmakers in town and if they'd call and want a pattern, a certain pattern or thread or something, their delivery boy would get on the bicycle and go and take it to the dressmaker. And that was a great convenience, of course. I remember one time being the youngest in the family, a great many of my clothes were made over. But I never seemed to mind because they were much nicer than material that would have been purchased probably for a little girl. But I remember one time I needed a new winter coat and we went to Creighton's and my mother and I looked for a coat for me. And I think it was probably the first ready made coat I had because it was red and it had a red velvet collar and I thought it was so beautiful. But my mother really felt it was more than she could afford to pay. But Mr. Creighton happened to come back where the coat was being shown. I think he knocked off a dollar or so and my mother purchased the coat for me because Mr. Creighton could see how badly I wanted that pretty red coat.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You wouldn't shop around in those days?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: The sales, my mother would go in and stock up on the number of sheets and tablecloths and so on she needed. And Mr. Creighton every year went to Ireland and bought linen. So even in the early days you could buy very beautiful linens here in Moscow. And like many homes, I suppose our home, we ate it on a white tablecloth three times a day. My father wouldn't have it otherwise and we all had white napkins and we never ate in the kitchen during my time. And sometimes for Sunday night supper I just thought it was so much fun when I was a child if we would have our supper in the kitchen and open the oven door. We were all sitting close together and it was such fun.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: In the book that I'm, you know, she mentions about how there were sort of grades of grocery stores, some stores. There were really, that's true. And there was a store down on Main Street near my father's place of business, O .C. Car Saw. And he would get in fruit and watermelons and things by, they didn't come in trucks, they came in hay wagons from Juliet and Lewiston and so on. And when he'd get in a load by everybody in Moscow would go down and get their fruit to camp. It would be stacked in boxes out on the sidewalk. And his prices were always supposed to be a little cheaper, but his family lived above the grocery store and I think he did most of the work himself so his overhead probably was lower and he could sell cheaper. I don't like to say any more about that store. But then there were other stores in town. Mr. Swan had a very nice store and I think, a grocery store, and I think it was considered very first class. Probably his line of canned goods and so on was a little more but a very fine quality. And then as the years would go by someone would start a grocery store and it would go a few years and they'd decide to close out or somebody would buy them out. So over the years there were quite a few grocery stores here as I remember.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well no, we had meat markets.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And I think we had at least, I remember three meat markets when I was a little girl. And that was one thing my father always purchased. He always bought the meat and it would be delivered to our home. He'd stop in as he went to work and choose the meat that my mother had wanted. And quite often I know we had round steak and oh we all, a family like that, he'd send home a great big round steak and I think perhaps it was only 25 or 35 cents. And they would, when it was prepared they'd pound it with a knife and then dip it in flour and fry it. It may not have been so good for us but it tasted awfully good. And then the drippings made delicious gravy over potatoes or biscuits and so on.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: At one time in Moscow we were supposed to have had as many saloons as we did churches. That was one thing they talked about. And I think there were 13 saloons and 13 churches. And we always, to go from our home, we always walked down 3rd street. And either side of the street we went on. We had to pass a saloon. And of course the men were standing around outside and they would chew tobacco and spit on the sidewalk. And I remember the ladies with their sweeping skirts in those days. Well you held up your skirt when you passed those places because it's so it wouldn't touch the dirty sidewalk. And of course then we had no paving and then every block was a crosswalk to get on the other side of the street. Well those crosswalks were quite high. I'd judge you 8 or 10 inches because the mud would get so deep you see in the winter. And it would rain. So they had to be high in order that you wouldn't sink clear in and lose your boots.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Those were all wooden planks. And the sidewalks were of wood. But I don't remember the paving came when the paving came but of course the main streets were paved first and cement sidewalks were put down. And of course in the residential neighborhoods that we had boardwalks, sidewalks we called them, long after the downtown streets had paved and cement sidewalks.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember what the feeling was at the time about saloons? About drinking?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well I think it was just an accepted thing for a man to drink. My father was never a drinker but I think many of what we'd consider the better class citizens drank. But my mother belonged to the WCTU and she was a very ardent worker and I can well remember when they would take petitions around to try to get the saloons out. At one time the prohibition party always was on the county ticket that I can remember. My father, we were all republicans always, but my mother one time they persuaded her to run on the prohibition party as state treasurer. Of course she didn't win but she took a great deal of teasing from my father over her experience in politics.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did she actually run a campaign?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh no, her name was just on the ticket. But then of course when the prohibition became the law why the saloons went out of course. Do you know what some of the things that the WCTU did in the early days to get the... I think that was just the most of it was just to carry petitions around and they had their meetings. I never belonged to this but I know my sister, one of my sisters would never and she and I are the only ones left. She doesn't even like to take a drink of wine because she signed a pledge when she was a little girl. They had an organization for the children. But during my time that never seemed to be...they didn't have that organization. But she said I signed that pledge and I don't feel right yet to even take a glass of wine. But I can't even remember what they...I wasn't in it so I don't even know what they... But I imagine they had children's meetings and talked to them about the evils of alcohol. Of course in our physiologies there was always a chapter in drinking alcohol and the harm it did to your body. I don't know that they have a physiology...I don't think they teach physiology that way today out of a book for children. It's part of their science but we had a book, a physiology book and there was always a chapter on the arms of alcohol to the human body.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think Moscow schools were always good schools and I think we had very good conscientious teachers. Some of them would be quite young when they'd start to teach but they were very anxious for Moscow children to learn. And in some ways I believe our learning in grade school was superior in some ways than what children get now. Although children know no more than we did because of their advantages of seeing and hearing and reading other books and so on. They have more knowledge but I don't think they actually learned the fundamentals as well as we did of education.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is there anything in particular that they've changed that you think?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well I don't know too much about the education now but I do know that my grandchildren with the new math, it takes them a great deal longer to figure out something than either their grandfather or their grandmother if it's a matter of dollars and cents. How many or something. Their way of going about it takes them much longer. Of course we're mature and we can see in a flash what it is you know.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well I guess I shouldn't tell that either.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I wanted to ask you a couple questions about stores too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there an ice cream parlor?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes indeed there was.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: In fact at one time I think there were more than two. But it was a great pleasure to go to the ice cream parlor and one of them had a table especially for children a little like a wrought iron or an iron table you know like porch furniture today with little chairs. And of course it was lovely to go in there and sit at that little table and we bought sundaes more than anything you know. We'd have ice cream with pineapple or strawberries or something over it and that was quite the thing.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they make their own ice cream?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes I think they did and it was very good ice cream too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would they have a lot of flavors?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well not too many flavors. It was mostly just vanilla and chocolate I think maybe strawberry some kind of times and then you could have it made into a sundae. But I remember one time before my husband and I were married we went into one of the confectionery stores and ice cream parlors that's what the way they usually called them because they made their candies too. And much like Mrs. Hunter makes the day their candies were you know.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And we used to in the winter we'd go in there and get oyster cocktail. Oh we just thought that was wonderful. It was cold you know why we'd like it in the winter I don't know but we did.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well it was oysters with raw oysters with a spicy sauce over it you know. I suppose the foundation of the sauce but we thought that was fun. When in like in our high school days we had lots of sleigh rides because our snow seemed to stay so much longer than it does now. And that was quite a popular form of entertainment in the winter to go on a sleigh ride party. And they'd hire either the livery stable or some farmer and he'd put a hay rack on runners you know and put straw in. We'd all take our blankets and sometimes we would go to Pullman and have oyster stew at some eating place over there you know. Of course that would take the whole evening to go over there and back. That was quite an entertainment we thought that was a lot of fun. Or we'd go to somebody who lived in the country and sometimes each of us took along some refreshments like a cake or something. Quite often they'd have hot cocoa for us you know. But I remember one time when I was in high school, Moscow was playing Genesee High School. And Mr. Neely took a group of us over. We started out right after school. And we drove over the mountain road because that well we just he went over that way. I don't know why it was shorter I think in order to get us there in time for the game. And we all were supposed to take along a sandwich to eat because we just would have to go to the game as quick as so we took our sandwich in a bag and ate it on the way over. And the game was played and it was in, I don't know whether it was in a warehouse but some, they didn't have a gym or anything you know. And while we were at the game a Chinook wind came up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know what they are?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes, a warm wind. And the snow all disappeared. And so we started home and Mr. Neely then came, the highway isn't as it is now but there was a more level road. And he came that way and from quite old four or five miles out from Moscow we all had to get out and walk because those horses couldn't pull, those runners wouldn't go you know pull with that many 20 or 25 young people in the wagon. And we, of course it took most of the night walking so far and everything because I know my mother wasn't concerned about me because I was going home to stay all night with one of my friends. And so when I came walking in about seven o 'clock in the morning she wondered why I came home so early and I told her we were just getting home from Genesee. But we had a bad misfortune out where the railroad tracks across down at South Main you know. The runner on the sled got caught in the track and Mr. Neely was driving a sled and we were all trailing behind of course because we were pretty weary by that time. And it knocked the sled being caught in the track, it threw him out and he hit his head and it knocked him out. Well we were all very frightened, I don't think he was knocked out for long but it was fortunate that he was so near his livery barn because you know. But that was one of them and I don't even remember who won the game after the experience of that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was this Brooklyn Game Master?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Basketball, they were playing basketball then. And Moscow, we were in that old old old high school you know that I told you about on 3rd Street and there was no place there of course. So they rented a warehouse down on Main Street where one of the grain companies is now, it was just a big wooden building you know and that's where they played basketball because I used to play girls basketball in that place.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was girls basketball a very popular thing?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well it was then and then it went out and now of course it's back again but we wanted, they had us play six girls to a team then. The rules were a little different than boys but we wanted to play boys rules but we weren't familiar.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you play around with different styles?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well I never was on the team I wasn't good enough but they did play other teams around. Mrs. Gayno was the center because she was so tall you know and she was Mabel Drury and they did play old different towns around. I don't think there were too many outside games played but and there weren't too many girls involved in the basketball program.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is there something you said?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: We just had to play skip to Malou and so on in the halls you know. They were that sort of party. But the school wouldn't let us use the building because we had to always ask some teacher to chaperone us. But the year I was a senior in high school there wasn't room for us to have a study hall so we just had to kind of stick around in the halls and keep our books on the windowsill some place you know. It was great fun but we didn't get any studying done in school time. We had to do our studying at home because we had no place to go. Although we did hang out down in the school superintendents office and how he ever stood 20. There were 20 in my class. All of us in there I'll never know but he was a very good natured man and they were the new building which is the office building on 3rd Street now was being built in and he was so, Mr. Randall was so wrapped up in that new building. I don't think he actually knew that we were he was over to the new building so much and everything you know that he actually knew we were just making use of his office like we did.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: So we sort of had open campus then the senior class.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What are some of the social events that you went to, parades?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh well 4th of July they made a great deal of 4th of July here with a big parade and the stores would have floats you know and the bands the band would play and then usually they would have several dance halls where the bands would play during the afternoon and evening. I remember 1 4th of July they had draped all the storefronts with bunting you know red, white, and blue bunting and it rained just terrifically. Well the bunting would drip down on all the white dresses you know but the dance halls were very popular that year because people even if they weren't dancing they just crowded until the floor space was very sparse because there were so many people standing around the edges to get out of the rain. And we had our well our special friends that we had parties in our homes you know I remember I belonged to a club and it I don't know there must have been 20 or 25 of us boys and girls and we called it the QAE club and that meant quit at 11. We had to go home at 11 that was the rule QAE and we were always saying well we wanted that we wished our mothers would let us change it to the QA T club 10 12 or 2 we thought that then we could stay till 12 you see that but we were grade schoolers there I would be junior high now. And those parties were in our homes I remember I had a Halloween party one time and we had it made a house full but of course with everybody came dressed masquerade you know and of course we had great fun pretending we didn't know people were and so on and the dipping for ducking for apples I guess you call it and all the biting the taking bites of a coat of a donut tied to a string and all the Halloween stuffs we used to do.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And I think there are many tricks played up all around.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well of course there were privies in those days you see and the bigger boys would tip over the privies or steal the gates off of the front gate off of the fence you know. There was always quite a bit of that so it was pretty wise to put your gate away for the evening but the privy there wasn't much you could do about that. But our next door neighbor was always so concerned my dad never seemed to be too concerned but he sort of patrolled his property the whole night I think and of course the kids knowing that would always do something to them. I don't remember being mixed up in anything very much. Oh we'd go out we didn't trick or treat that was that we just didn't know about that here in Moscow then but we'd go out we'd make tic tacs we'd not just spool and my brother usually made mine and put it on a little stick or a pencil and then wrap a string around it and go up to someone's window and pull a string you know and of course the room would be there. And the racket would the spool would turn and make quite a racket or we'd ring somebody's doorbell and run that was the extent of our mischief I think. But we just didn't know about knocking on the door and saying trick or treat. But we usually I don't know why we went if somebody didn't have a party we'd go out with our jack -o'-lanterns around our own neighborhoods of course but we didn't expect them to give us anything at all. I think one of the nicest childhood parties I remember was the one that Carol Riery's mother gave her and she shipped in the ice cream on the that came on the noon train from Spokane as I told you and it was all had been molded in like little animals. And we had rabbits or chickens or so on and we thought that was the most elegant party because we had never seen ice cream molded like that before.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that QAE club? Was that at all formal?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh no no. Oh no we had a membership. Yes it was a membership club and I think there were about 20 to 25 of us and we never went home with a boy or anything we just all go in groups you know. And the fellow that lived the farthest I suppose he had the 12th last part of the way home by himself.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you dance?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well we did. We didn't learn as we didn't go to dances like children do now even in high school. But I don't know if we had older brothers and sisters we just gradually learned how to dance at home you know.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I have a terrible story to tell but I don't know whether you would want to tape it or not. It was when we were in I don't believe we were in well we must have been in high school but we had a leap year Sunday school party and the girls class in the Presbyterian Sunday School and the boys class about our ages they were about the same size. So each girl asked a boy to go to this leap year party. Well there was a woman teacher the boys class and we had a man teacher the girls class and so we went to this girl was a niece living with her aunt that year going to school. It was the day home we went there to the party. That was the big home of the city. And so we had sort of made a plan that after the party was over in the refreshments we'd all start home and then we'd go back and dance. And it always bothered me and I don't believe our nice Sunday school teacher ever knew what we had done because this girl her aunt was away and she'd ask the next door a girl a young lady and her fiance to come in and chaperone. But our dancing was very hoppity I imagine.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But that always bothered me because this Sunday school teacher he was such a peach and we'd have parties Sunday school parties and he played the piano and that was a great thing to do in those days to stand around the piano and sing you know. Well he'd play all these popular songs sheet music you know and we'd sing and he was so good to play for us to sing and we treated him that way. So it always kind of bothered me. He was always one of my good friends but I had a guilty feeling.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Are there any other you know circuses?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh yes. That was a big day when the circus came to town and of course they traveled by train and we'd go have an alarm call and awaken us. And some of several friends we'd get together and go down to watch the train come in and it usually came in around four o 'clock in the morning and it'd be cold you know. But it was very interesting and they would take off the elephants first because they had to pull the big poles and the tents, the canvases to wherever the circus was to be. And we had several big open spaces quite close downtown in those days where the circus could be. And so after we'd watched the circus wagons all painted you know beautifully painted and of course the animals were inside we could hear the lions and the tigers and so on. They weren't open because we weren't supposed to see them until. We'd hurry and go out to the circus grounds because if they carried water to the elephants by pails and that's the way they had to do it from just hydrants you know. Nearby hydrants where they'd get a ticket to the circus. And Harry did that and I know my brother used to do that and he'd get a ticket to the circus. But even though we didn't have too much money that was one thing my father thought we should always go to the circus. He thought that was a very important thing for us to do. So there was always money for us to go to the circus and we'd always go early because they had the animals in one big tent with the cages around the sides you know and you could walk around and look at them before you went into the main tent to take your seat. And of course I don't remember of ever sitting in a reserved seat which you had a little back and was right at the center ring. These were usually three ring circuses but you'd try to get as near that as you could find a seat you know so you thought you could see better. And then they opened the circus with the grand entry you know the parade of the Bespangled Girls riding on the elephants heads and the riders on the horses and the good and the cages coming around. But in the morning a thing which they never do anymore they'd have a parade on Main Street and that was one reason that they came to town so early and of course many children that would be all they'd get to see of the parade because the country people would drive in for the parade. I think it was usually scheduled for ten o 'clock and it never came along until about eleven and that was the one day of the year that my father didn't come home for the new meal. So my mother would oh I thought this was lovely she'd buy buns and bologna when we'd go to town and we'd have cookies or something and we'd go to my father's business and eat. But every other working day of the year he came home for his new day meal but circus day because of the parade he didn't expect anyone to stay home to get him a meal. And oh that was very thrilling to have the parade get on Main Street.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would it only be one circus?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes one.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh well no sometimes we used to have ringling circus wasn't first wasn't with Barnum and Bailey you know and we didn't get both big ones every year. But then there was some and they were talking about it on what's my line the other night. I forgot my name that was smaller circus that used to come and then we used to have dog and pony shows come. Well they just had dog and pony performers and they were delightful shows. They'd have a band but oh the ponies and would perform you know and they had white ponies and little dark ponies and shetland ponies no horses. And then the little dogs you know would go through all their antics jumping through hoops and of course we love dog and pony shows I think there I think it's too bad children don't get to see them now because they're really a rather tranquil show you know for a child to see. Then they were delightful. One thing that the circus is used to have and it was quite the highlight for me they would have the statues have you ever had heard of that. Well in one end of the tent they'd have this big thing all covered with white canvas or muslin or something. And then at this time of the show the big lights would focus on that and they'd raise this and here would be about five performers in tights all perfectly white their faces and all and they'd pose as the three graces or something you know. And the band would play appropriate music and then the cover would down come down and they'd pose for another and to me that was just most exquisite. Well I don't think they ever do that anymore but that was really quite oh yes. And of course they always had nets below them but then the crowd would always go oh well you know when they would catch somebody somersaulting through the air you know. I never remember seeing anyone heard at all because they always use nets. Of course I think some of the performers today especially European surfaces don't use as much careful care about their they're so sure of themselves let me say they're so much better trained perhaps.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever see medicine shows?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes I remember one that came to town and it's right where Shorts Chapel is now and it went through all I don't know what was the medicine they sold or anything but they had this sort of like a minstrel show don't you know two or three actors. That would put on and then the salesman would go through the crowd to dispose of the medicine. I don't remember that the Moody family ever bought any I can't remember that we did and I don't I think it was just mostly children who went to the medicine show. And then we had. They just put on a little. Yes just a tiny little stage build up you know just skits and then. In the. The hypnotist would come to town sometimes and they'd always. Put somebody to sleep in their store window you know with just something under their neck and something under their heels and they would be suspended. You know they'd put a great I think at one time they got a big block of granite from my father to go on this hypnotized person's stomach and. But that was great and then at a certain time perhaps they'd have a show the hypnotist would in the whether we had the opera house or the G .A .R. Hall or wherever it was he can talk and wake them from the stage you see. The complex there with. But he'd awaken them from the. From the stage and you do all of his disappearing tricks you know through the trunk and so on. Then. We used to have should talk was here at one time. And. Well we did in Moscow too. And I was I just I was of high school age then and it lasted a week. And it was morning and afternoon and old people would just flock to the should talk well you know they put up in our city park. And it would be there at the stock would be and you bought your ticket for the whole week. I don't think they sold tickets for each event I think you had. And I don't know who. Oh yes oh yes we went. We thought we were very well educated. They'd have lectures you know and then maybe a musical group. It was all brought in talent. And. Oh I don't even remember that but. Were they at all religious. Well no I don't believe they were religious. Margaret Shimky gave a program the other night on. A revival here. And I'd forgotten all about it. But it seems all the Protestant churches had gone in on this and. Right back to the old post office where the old Christian church used to be at the parking lot. They build a wooden tabernacle they called it just a board you know. And she said in her story the other night that she went every night after school he had a children's well I think I was enough older that I'm enough older than Margaret that because I never went there to any children's meeting. I don't even remember of going at all but. Margaret said she and Marguerite often went every night after school and Lily and Otnes to this children's meeting but I think I was probably enough older that I would look down on that sort of thing you know I was too big.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I remember the.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well I don't. I don't think Presbyterians ever went in so much for that sort of thing as some other denominations. I don't believe we did. So I don't know that's kind of a blank but. I thought maybe when she gave this paper she was. Talking about where she lived before she came to Moscow but she said no. That was right here in Moscow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh yeah do you remember when Armistice died?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh yes of course I was a young woman then you see and the false armistice came first you see and we all went downtown and then learned that it wasn't true.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did you hear about it?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well I don't know we didn't have radios I don't know how we knew maybe. Probably because everybody went downtown but then I don't know how many days it wasn't many days before the probably there were rumors don't you know that it was to be signed. And then on the real armistice they we all went downtown and. I think my sister my mother died in 1918 and my sister and my older sister and I were at home with my dad and she'd been sick and I think probably she had what we call flu now. And she was ill most of the winter from going to that she had a relapse from this but that's that's another interesting thing. The university was a barracks you see for to train soldiers for several years here and when that flu epidemic came. They just I know I represented they asked me to do the calling for the Red Cross in our neighborhood and I went from door to door and got blankets and pillows. And they asked the people were all asked to mark them so they get them back because they had boys in the gymnasium even in one of the livery stables here and the Elks Tampa sick boys and some and Harry was in David's store then and they had to buy their own. Uniform I guess just the officers had to buy their own uniforms and he would go up to take their measurements he was a clothing man and he was supposed to wear a mask well it was so much trouble that he'd take the mask off but he never got it but several he took measurements for and they died before their uniforms even came. It was a terrible thing and one of the big homes here in Moscow the Emmy Lewis home they used as a kitchen and I women went in would prepare the soup and so on the transporter of course we had cars by then around to these places where the sick boys were but so many died. It was a terrible thing.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And was it mainly just the army?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well it was Army ROTC the infantry was what was here.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And they're the main ones that got the things? Isn't that terrible?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Of course in that we had ROTC here in World War II but then we had all branches of service and accommodations were much better and those things didn't happen then. But in that first World War the women knitted you know and it was because I was young and foot free and so on I would carry yarn around to the women who knitted and they knitted socks and gloves and mufflers. No the Red Cross brought the yarn in and it was given out to and I know I'd take it to so many of the Scandinavian women knitted you know and I lived in that part of town so I would carry in the directions they had.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But those women were such good knitters and well what service?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That means we must quit talking. No it doesn't mean that. It means you have something to keep you going. There are a plate of cookies out there and it's Lola's recipe and they're kind of they aren't like Lola's that Mrs. McComill's.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did Lola or Clyde ever give that to you?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Her recipe. Mrs.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: McComill's recipe that lived in the mansion? Would you like it?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Sure. Say there were more than that a good deal when they started but these are those. Now Lola's I pressed up. I went from an outside water hydrant to carry water into the house to be heated in a coal range with a water front in it to heat the water and then put in a reservoir to keep it hot. To indoor the water brought inside and then a hot water tank heated by the kitchen range and with sewer and from an outside toilet to an inside toilet and a bathroom with a bathtub. Before that it was we never bathed in tubs as I remember we had a big bath pan that was brought in and that bath pan always hung out in the buttery. Now you don't know how a buttery is. Well my mother's father was brought up in New England and they all had butteries and it was just a place to keep the supplies that were kept like flour and sugar and that sort of supplies. The big supply and then you'd carry it into the smaller containers in the kitchen. And then I went from kerosene lamps to electricity and our first lights were just a drop guard you know with a bulb on the end of it before we had any fixtures. But the lamps, the coal oil lamps, that was a hard time. My mother, my father was great on having every room lighted in the house and so that meant and maybe several lights like in our we didn't call it we called it the front room then it was the living room. Several lamps there and then in the room, the dining room probably where the studying was done at night around the dining room table you know. And she had at least 10 or 12 lamps every morning to fill and wash the chimneys and trim the wicks and it took one hour of her morning every morning to prepare the lamps for the evenings lighting session.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: The chimneys had to be cleaned you know, no land or anything on them, they had to be sparkling.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Am I on still?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And then we burned in the front room, the living room, we had a great huge heater. The wood had to be brought in every night after school and under one kitchen table, it wasn't the work table but another kitchen table was filled with wood every night for the heater. But then finally we started burning coal so it just meant getting in the scuttles of coal.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But that fell, that job fell to me after my brother was, had gone from home. I was the youngest so I fell heir to the job of bringing in the wood and mowing the lawn in the summer.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was the wood split already?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, a regular cordwood, that's the way my father bought it, he bought 9 or 10 cords every fall, it was stacked in her alley. I think it was about 4 foot lengths. And then that was, the wood sawer came in with a mechanical saw and sawed it. And then it was carried into the woodshed, we had a great big woodshed and stacked clear to the ceiling. To supply us, that supplied us until wood had to be purchased the next summer. But of course, kindling would have to be split for the kitchen stove to ignite. Well before we burned coal, the wood had to be split for the kitchen stove. Because there was a wood box we had to fill behind the stove.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Before Sam came in, you were talking about going around for the Red Cross? Did most people here really support the war?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh yes, oh yes.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And as I say, those Scandinavian people, they didn't, I couldn't converse with them, but they knew what we needed. And boy did they turn out the socks and things. And a lot of women couldn't, they had kitchener toe and heels, it was a special toe and a heel. And the heel was kind of like this, you know, shape, not just, and a lot of people couldn't do those. And so I know my mother used to, I'd bring them to my mother after the long leg was, and I think they made them pretty long. And she'd put in the kitchener toe and heel.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you take out what had been done?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, no, she'd just go right on and finish the foot, you see, the heel and the foot. It's up there. Their knitting wouldn't have been just the same, but she did that for many.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Are there other things that the...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: About the Red Cross?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, Mrs. Laney was head of the Red Cross here then, and she just turned her home over. They made hospital gowns out of muslin. I don't know, I never got in on that part because I was just a young woman and I didn't know about that, but I was a good Erin girl.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But then in churches, different churches, they'd roll bandages instead of a woman's, a lady's aide or something, meaning the time was spent rolling bandages. Moscow responded very well. In later years, I was secretary of the Red Cross here, and Moscow always responded well to the Red Cross. Well, I believe maybe one reason... Well, I don't know either. Dean Eldridge was in the YMCA. In that first World War, that was such a big thing, you know, the YM's Young Men's Christian Association. And he was sent to France. But there was just... There were so many local people in it here that Moscow felt a great interest. And I think the things that were accomplished, Moscow wasn't near as big then, were really quite remarkable for a town of this size. And this being the county seat, you see, it went out to all the county from here for people. In every town, there'd be a woman who would set it up and tend to it. The response was splendid.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That was a night -tea. Oh, I know. I was going to ask you about the lady's aide.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did the lady say, too? What is a lady's aide?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, in our church, there was...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: The lady's aide, that really was just the organization in the church that kind of tended to the local wants of the church, you know. Gave ice cream socials and so on to raise money to augment the minister's salary, or if they needed something in the Sunday school. The whole other money was used for that.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And then we had a minister's wife who felt that that wasn't sufficient. Well, Mrs. Gormley. And so she started the Women's Missionary Society. Well, the women began to think. A lot like to go to the lady's aide. That was more or less social, you see. And when they put on these special events, they all liked to work at them. But nobody wanted to belong to the missionary society. And so she got the bright idea of combining them. So it's been that way in our church to this day. It's all one organization's. Of course, it's that way nationally now in my church, the Presbyterian Church. But Mrs. Gormley had a very far vision by putting those into one organization. Because she made the missionary society as strong as the lady's aide then, you see, because they all belonged to both.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would it be most of the women in the church?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Because that was a big part of the social life of the town.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Later on, clubs started, various women's clubs, but in the early days that was the socialite of the town. Socialite.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would each church have something to say?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. That was very...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: It wasn't just the Presbyterian Church?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, no. They all had their women's organization. And I think a good many ministers might have been hungry, you know, if those women hadn't helped raise money on the side to help off the church budget.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there other things about the church that you remember from the earlier years?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes. Of course. My mother was a great church woman, and my father always went to church, too. He made a Presbyterian of her. She'd been brought up, something else.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But we all went... My mother had six children. I was a baby. And I know she used to say she started taking me to church when I was six months old to Sunday school. She went... Believed in parents going to Sunday school. I don't remember my father ever going, but she managed to get six children ready and to get to Sunday school herself. And so we were brought up just to think that was the thing to do. And then my father would come on to church service at 11, you see. But Sunday school was earlier, and then he would come on and join my mother at church.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did she always do that?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Always.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Always.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would many of the other parents?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Quite a few. I think, like the Davids, they were always... all went to Sunday school, and many of the families in the church did that. That was just a part of church life, too. And they're coming back to that now, which is very nice.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there other activities for the young people? In the church?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, yes. Now, my older brothers... or my oldest brother and my older sisters, they had a Christian endeavor, which was a young people's organization that met in the evening. And then they had... that was their social life to a great extent. Of course, on Sunday night it was their religious service of swords, you know. And then they'd have parties during the week. That was the social life of the town. You belonged to the Christian endeavor. It was called Other Things in Other Churches, but in my church, I had another cup of coffee.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Was there... is that...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: did you belong to that, too?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I never did.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I had stopped by the time I come on. And, well, you see, by that time we were having other interests. Being the tail end of the family, you know. I... I was developing like... I always went to Sunday school, but we didn't have any of this sort of... young people's meetings in my time.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But later on, my sister, not the one who's living in Lonesome, but the next one who always made her home with us, Myra, went into religious education, and she was the director in our church here, and she started that again. The minister saw the need from the campus of that. And so they... it was started again. And she started Westminster Guild for Young College Girls, but really this is outside the scope of this what you want. But that was considered an extra -curricular activity on the campus, and it was accepted as that, and girls who participated in that got credit for their extra -curricular activity, like in sororities and things.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did people from the universities always come down to the church?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes. Um -hmm.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Um -hmm. And back, I think now there isn't... although they're coming real well at our church, but they do have... like the Catholics have their center on the campus, and they have a Christian center on the campus, and I think the young people pretty much go to those. And then they have during the week these bullfests, or bull, whatever they call them.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That's out of the realm of what you want. That's out of the realm of what you want. Quite small, because we had gone on a camping trip up near Bovill, and I remember I was this small that in the morning I rode in the horse and buggy with my mother and father, and they carried me out in my nightgown because it was early when we started, you see. So I must have been. Not too big. But we had our camping trip out there, and as we came home in two weeks, it was a Sunday, and we got out... there was a place halfway out there, and a Negro family lived there. It was kind of a halfway house, and the people used to stop on the way... the white pines, we called it, up around Bovill, you know. People had timber claims up there. In fact, Carol writes about that in her book. And so they told us, or I think somebody drove up while we were there with a team of horses, and told us what had happened in Moscow. So of course we were all very much excited about it. Well, by the time we got in there, the house where it happened was right down here, where this service station caddy cornered. And of course, that was the only road into town, and the highway wasn't here. So we had to go right by it. And of course, all traces of the tragedy were gone by then. But of course, there were bullet holes in the house. I don't know that I actually saw them, but that was what they said. But that happened very much as it was in the book. And this man who went berserk had been told by Dr. Watkins that he thought he should be committed to Orofino, to the asylum, because he beat up and was so cruel to his mother. And this man's father, I think, had been a mason along with Dr. Watkins. And so Dr. Watkins was sort of taking him, I don't know how old he was, under his, you know, trying to do what was the right thing. So he just went berserk that particular day, and Dr. Watkins went to his office every Sunday morning.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And he shot him, came up on his horse and shot Dr. Watkins. But the horse just kept going and went down and stopped in front of his offices. It was used to doing, you see. That was, so matter of fact, the horse just did this thing. It was its habit to do. And pretty soon somebody saw Dr. Watkins slumped, and then this fellow went on down the street on his horse and Mr. Creighton, who owned the store where Creighton's is now. He always carried a cane, not for need, but for looks and so on. And he shot at Mr. Creighton. I don't know what his grudge was again, Mr. Creighton, but he just hit him in the arm. And then he went on down and came up 8th Street to where a deputy sheriff lived. And he stopped there and asked for a drink of water. And he had intended to shoot the deputy sheriff, but he had this baby in his arms. And I know those girls real well. They don't live there anymore. So he didn't shoot him. And then he had another, oh, there were several men on his list. He came, as he came in toward the Predator in church, he came in 8th Street, what 8th Street there is now, and stopped at the Held home, which I know, because I was born in that neighborhood. And Mrs. Held said, Mr. Held was not home. So he went on then and got Dr. Watkins. And so by the time this had all happened, Posse had gotten together, and they'd opened the hardware stores and got guns for all the men. And they came out by foot, of course, because he'd come out to his home and barricaded himself in the upstairs. And so they just poured the shot into that house, and finally his old mother came out and said, don't shoot anymore. He's dead. And one man in Moscow claimed that he'd fired the bullet that killed him, you know, but nobody ever knew.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now that's in, which one in Carol's books? Have you read any? Was that in Buffalo Code?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That's why I thought.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that mainly where you're remembering from?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, I remember that. Well, of course, it's mainly from Carol's book and then our talk in the home when I was a child, you see. But I remember going on that camping trip, although I don't remember any of the details, but I remember coming back and going by that house.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there ever any other things like that that happened? Well, of course, was it in Buffalo Code where the minister's daughter went to Orofino?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yeah, that was in the same story.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That actually happened, do you know?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: The minister's daughter went down to Orofino with his doctor and murdered suicide. No,

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I don't remember of anything real tragic like that ever happening.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: There was, oh, there were farm accidents and things that were bad sometimes, you know, but nothing that was just actual planned out like this was.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Were there ever any trials that were... Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Of course, in the old courthouse, what was that trial?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But that was after this time you were wanting. But there were trials here. And then the federal court was here, you see, and the Indians were always brought here for their misdemeanors because they had to appear in federal courts, you see. And that was one of the talking points against our old post office has always been the Indians didn't like to climb those stairs up to the federal court. That always tickled me.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, I wanted to...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go to a dentist?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. I think Harry had dentists on his list. But dentistry, the dentist we went to, I think he must have been a pretty crude dentist. But he was a big man and he had such huge hands. I don't know. And it was old styled dentistry, of course. But I remember once I had to have one of my molars pulled and I just fought that man terribly. And he finally, I just wiggled out of his chair and he pulled a tooth. I was on the floor when he pulled a tooth. But he gave me the tooth and I know I had it in a little box for years. But we didn't know anything about the tooth fairy then either. But dentistry was so crude and it hurt so.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What would they do? What kind of thing?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, I don't think they ever... Of course, I was so young then I was just... I think they just pulled the teeth. But later on, this would still be in the time you want, I guess, the dentist we came to, he was... By then they were putting cocaine or I don't think it was the oval cane, but something in, you know, if they did work. But his procedure on dentistry was if you got a cavity in a tooth, was to extract the nerve of the tooth and then fill it. And I know he, even though he used that, they'd run this little wire in and twist it around the nerve somewhere and jerk it out. And you just felt one terrible excruciating pain, you know. And of course the tooth was dead then. Because I know I had a dead tooth here in the front eye before I got dentures that finally gave me trouble. But that was his method, to extract the nerve from the tooth and then fill it. Well, he ran a little wire, he'd drill and then put this in and pull a nerve out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Doesn't that sound crude?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, it was crude, but he was considered a very good dentist. I remember him so well because he hummed all the time he worked on you. His daughter was one of my best friends, so...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I know when my mother had, he extracted my mother's teeth and my father didn't, was going to be up there to be with her during the ordeal. And he was late, something happened and he didn't get there in time. And just as he came in the waiting room door, the dentist's waiting room door, you heard, Dr. Watkins, his name was Watkins, too, that was no relation. He said, I'll never pull another tooth for Mrs. Wooty. And it scared my father to death, he thought she'd died or something, you know. And he just, man, he'd finished the job. But she was very uncomfortable, I remember, after that. And then they waited several months before they put the new teeth in, you know. Because I know when I had to have my upper teeth extracted, they put the teeth in immediately because they'd taken the mold already and made them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that on TV? Or on?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yeah, that's right. Can you tell me about your grandmother who lived out on... Oh, yes.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, that was the grandmother that, that was my mother's mother who lived in the Salubria, where I said my mother came to. And she married Mr. Smith, we always called him, we never called him grandpa or anything but Mr. Smith. And they bought a place down on Fix Ridge. And Grandma was just sort of the do -gooder on that ridge. Anybody was sick, they'd call Grandma because she had all these old -time remedies, you know. And anybody died, Grandma had to go and help lay them out, as she called it. But they had a, they did have phones, but they had a signal that if anything happened, the Central rang this one ring and everybody took their phones down. So they would know whatever had happened, you know. And that always seemed like that was quite neat to me when I was a child. But we'd drive down, Mama went down several times during the good year, good part of the year, good weather. And I was a little girl and I went with her. And we had to ford a little stream out here someplace beyond Lenville. And my mother, we'd start out early in the morning because it took a sprinter all day to get there because the horse never went at a very good gate for my mother, kind of walked all the way. And my mother always told me I'd be having a nap when we came to the stream because it, as I remember, you could just, it was just like that. But it scared me anyway, bumping across the rocks. And I've never found the place where that was, but of course it's so different now. But then I'd get out at the top of the hill above Grandma's house. They lived kind of down in the low little valley. And go down through the arch and beat Mama there, of course, because she'd have to go around and go through the lane to get to the house. But Grandma had feather beds and we didn't have feather beds, we just had mattresses. And oh, I thought that was just heaven to sleep in a feather bed. Oh yes, we'd stay several days when we'd go like that. Because it was an all -day trip almost for my mother to get there and we'd stay several days. And I know we often went when the peaches were right down there. And Grandma, they had quite a prune orchard in them. They had quite a business of drying prunes. That was quite the thing here in the early days. People would grow prunes and dry them. And I think they must have sold them to the stores. But they had a house dryer where they had some kind of fire. I don't know what it was. And the prunes would be put in on chicken wire racks or something and they'd dry them. I don't know why they didn't taste smoky, but they didn't. They were delicious. Because we always had a sack hanging in the buttery when I was a child. We'd play sort of Cops and Robbers, you know. The buttery was always the jail, but it was kind of an advantage to be in jail because you could eat dried prunes and dried apples.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your mother dry the apples for some?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes, I remember she did. She put sheets up on the Woodhouse roof and we'd spread. Just think of peeling all those apples.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And then my brother probably was set up and they would spread them out and then they'd put a net, what is it, they used window netting, what they call that?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Mosquito net. Mosquito net, uh -huh, over them. Keep the bugs off. But they never thought about the dust. Because our street wasn't paved and the dust would get about that deep in the summer and a team of that vehicle happened to go by and the dust would roll in. But we always sprinkled down our road, or the street in front of our house, but several of the neighbors did. But beyond this, they never sprinkled, so we always managed to get quite a bit of dust. Because that was one job I had to do was sweeping off the veranda every day and I had a job all that dust. But people then used to sit out on their porch in the evenings. You know, it was hot, but you sat out there and the neighbors would come over and visit. My father always sat out ready's paper in the evening until it got too dark to read. But you used your porch as a great deal in those days. And so that was one of my jobs, was to sweep off the veranda every morning.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have other jobs?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, filling the wood box and then sprinkling the lawn.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How would you do that?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, we had a sprinkler, but to get the edges, I would hold the hose and sprinkle. And I know if I was anxious to go someplace, if I'd make it look awfully wet on the sidewalks and everything, my father would think I'd done a pretty good job of sprinkling so I could get going. But actually, it was just all surface. But I was really supposed to start the hose late in the afternoon and give the lawn a good watering.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have much of a garden?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. We had these two lots, you see, south of our house, and there were trees, and then in the center was all open with a garden spot. And we always had our own potatoes and peas and beans and things and corn. And my father used to hire the next door neighbor to come over and spade it up for him, and then he'd plant it, and every evening he'd go out in the hole and work in the garden.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And we, it was always a delight to go down in our fruit cellar in the fall after everything was harvested. There were sacks and lapels and sacks of potatoes and all the shells full of canned fruit. And because the stores, you just could only buy oranges and bananas in the winter, you know, that was all you could ever buy. We didn't even have head lettuce or anything like that in the winter then. But we had our own beets and carrots, and they were stored, and parsnips, and all the things that you could grow that would keep, you know.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But I don't remember, we never grew our own tomatoes in those days. We always had to buy them, but several gardeners around town would, out in the country, grew tomatoes. But I don't know why we never thought we could have tomatoes in our garden. We thought they wouldn't write when, you know, the frost came. But, of course, I think the varieties are better now and earlier and so on. And so we can ripen tomatoes easily in Moscow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you, did you can any meat or anything like that?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, we never did. We never did that. I know lots of people did. We did, what we did do in the spring of the year, my grandmother would always give us two old pigs. And we had, our lot was big, so we had a place, a pig pen, you know. And we had our own chickens. But then in the early winter, later than this, when it started to get real cold, my father'd have a man come and butcher them. But they never smoked the meat. I think it was packed down in barrels with salt. And so that meant it always had to be parboiled, you know, it'd be salty. But it was good. But I don't know what about the hams and things. They made sausage. But I don't know what, I don't remember whether they just had to be used fresh or how they used them because they weren't smoked. Unless my father, this man took them and smoked them. He must have because we'd have bacon. So this man who did the butchering must have smoked the meat at his own.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it unusual for family in the city to have a big garden and, you know, chicken?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, not in our part of town, it wasn't unusual, but down near Main Street, people didn't usually have that sort of thing. My friends, I don't think, that lived nearer in. One's father was a doctor and the other's was a blacksmith, and they never did have at their homes. Because the blacksmith's shop, or the black girl who had the father as a blacksmith lived for a safe way is now their home was there. And his shop was right where the theater is, there, the movie theater. And then Dr. Critters had a, later he had a hospital over where the apartments are on 1st Street across from the United Church down there. But their home was up on 1st Street and they had no garden. They had a barn, they had a horse, but they had no garden. So I don't believe as a rule, people who lived closer in, we lived in what was considered quite far out, and then having that big a lot, why we always had a garden. And it was probably a necessity with six children to have a garden. And then Mr. Ramstead, who lived near us at Sal and Ramstead, who's in the cradons, his grandfather, they lived right across the alley, back of us. And he had a great big orchard, and we bought cherries. I know he'd bring over great milk pails of cherries and my mother would can those because we had cherry pie, an apple pie and so on. But lots of cherry pie and mincemeat pie and so on in the winter. But I know Mrs. Fyle, who was Aunt Elsie in Carol's book, who brought Carol up, used to say, my oldest sister never could go any place on Saturday because she had to make cherry pies. And she said she made seven. Well, I can't even imagine one family making seven cherry pies at a time. But Mrs. Elsie said that was the way it was at the Moody House, so I don't know, I don't remember that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever eat out? Eat out?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, no, not, no, we didn't. Actually, the hotels were the only places who had dining rooms. I don't remember being taken to the hotel to eat ever when I was a child. If you went to another town state in the hotel, then you did. If you went to Spokane or anything, you know, but being in Moscow, I don't remember ever eating at the hotel as a little child.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Of course, you went to church suppers and, as I told you before, the friends always took me to the beanbake and the GAR supper and so on. So those were the way you ate out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was the beanbake?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, the GAR is the Grand Army, the Republic, the Civil War veterans used to have that. And these friends, the father belonged, so they always took me along with them.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: You said when you were talking about that, that those were the old times. They were, we thought they were, because of the men all had their like, marriage and so on.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And we thought they were really, well, they were really the people who settled around here first, those people.

speaker: Laura Schrager
