words: Do you remember any of the, you know, any of the older people that impressed you in somehow?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, oh.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You know, the early settlers?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, Mr.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Naylor was one and, of course, his daughter was my friend, but I thought he was pretty fine. And then they had a friend, and I think he was no bachelor, Mr. McBean, who had this great white beard. And I always was a little in awe of him, you know, with this great white beard. But I don't, of course, now Harvey Smith's father, Mr. H .R. Smith, was always superintendent of our Sunday school. And I think he had a smaller beard, and I think we all stood in a little awe of him. But much after I was a little child, he was superintendent so many years, some little child went home and told their mother that they'd seen God that day at Sunday school, and they called Mr. H .R. Smith God. That's Harvey's father, whom you're going to talk to tomorrow. He was a lawyer.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But in those days, the university wasn't so big, and the town wasn't so big, and there was much sociability and good feeling between... We always went to things on the campus, even when I was a small child. Of course, the things were recitals and declamatory contests and that sort of thing, debates, you know. But the townspeople always went to those things. That was the thing that we all did. And I think that was a good feeling we had. And the women of the town, the women who lived on that side, on the university side, would have their afternoon at home one day a week, and the women who lived on this side would stay at home for college, you see. And they'd have their little tea tables and serve tea. Well, the women who had friends on this side would come and call. They did a great deal of calling in those days. And the first time you went to call on anyone, you always left your card, you see. They always had a little tray or plate near the door, and you'd always leave your card. Of course, even after I was married, people called on me and left their cards.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: The women would have cards?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Uh -huh, with their names on. This is so and so so. But that was rather a nice custom. We don't take time to call like that much anymore. And it was a nice custom. Of course, our groups of friends are so much larger now that you just don't do that sort of thing, but it's too bad, especially for newcomers who come to town. They have to sort of make their own means of getting acquainted, you know, it seems, by going to church or perhaps, or asked to join some club or something. But I think that was a very good idea. Of course, when I was a little girl, we thought it was pretty nice when we were asked to serve punch at the university dances. They'd fix up a real, like the junior prom or something, a beautiful table, and ask several little town girls to come up to serve the punch. And we thought that was really something to be asked to come to university to serve punch at a dance.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What would the dance be like? Would they get real dressed up?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. They really dressed up. The girls had evening gowns, and I don't think they wore the men wore, they didn't wear tuxedos or faux dress or anything, but they had suits, the men wore suits. But we had cabs, horse cabs in those days, and so if a young man could afford it, he'd take each girl, especially if she lived across town, to the dance in a cab. But they always picked the little girls up. That was part of the expense of those who were the class that was giving the dance to get the little girls there who served, and we thought that. Only I was always the last one home, because we lived the farthest away, and my mother didn't appreciate that very much.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But I think those of us who had older brothers and sisters in college, we really had two educations, because we knew everything that was going on when we were children, you know, that they'd come home and talk about.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And they, when we got older ourselves and did the same sort of thing, we sort of lived it before, don't you know?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: One time, maybe I told you this before, we were over to a party. It was about this time of the year, to a little friend's house, and it got dark early, and it was just pouring rain, and my two little friends and I were coming home now, and we lived on this side. And this big tall man came along and asked us to walk under his umbrella. He always carried a great big umbrella. He was from Canada, rather English. We didn't know who the man was, but oh, he asked us so many questions, what our names were, and if we had brothers and sisters in college and so on. And afterwards we found out it was the new president of the university, Dr. McLean, and here we told the whole life history of our brothers and sisters who were in college, and they were pretty put out with us, because the school wasn't so big then, and of course he knew who they were when they heard their names.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I expect he wondered why three little girls were out around five o 'clock on such a date. He ate his dinner, the hotel, he was going for his dinner.
